title: Which Streaming Service Was That On Again?
excerpt: Words from me as developer, student and human.
content_html:
I'm a huge movie fan. I'd even call myself a cinephile. The films I enjoy most are the ones that leave me thinking afterwards. What I don't want to think about beforehand is where I can actually watch them. These days, movies are spread across what feels like a hundred different streaming services. I still don't understand why there isn't a better built-in solution for this. Or maybe I just don't want to accept that this is the only option. Either way, I wanted to figure out how we ended up here, so I did some research.

The original promise of streaming was simple. Everything would be available whenever you wanted it. You could sit down in the evening and binge an entire season without waiting a week for the next episode.

Well... weekly releases seem to be making a comeback. I've forgotten more than one show simply because I had to wait a week for the next episode. But overall, streaming still changed the way we watch movies and TV. 

I still remember recording TV shows because otherwise you'd miss them, or waiting until 8:15 p.m. (the prime time) because that's when the big movie of the week started. Today you just lie down in bed, open an app, and watch whatever you're in the mood for. Broadcast schedules don't matter anymore. 

Another huge improvement was the lack of ads. One of the most annoying things about television was the ad breaks every fifteen minutes. Early streaming didn't have that. You paid for a Netflix subscription and that was it. 

It was also usually cheaper than cable or pay TV, although I'm honestly not sure that's still true today. And maybe the biggest advantage of all was that one streaming service was enough. Most of the movies and shows people wanted to watch were in the same place, so you never had to think about who owned the rights.

Then everything started to fragment. Over time, it felt like every major studio launched its own service. Then Netflix proved just how profitable streaming subscriptions could be. Instead of licensing their movies to Netflix, studios realized they could keep the content, launch their own platform, and collect the subscription fees themselves. From a business perspective, it made perfect sense. From a viewer's perspective, it was the beginning of the mess. Disney made Disney+, Warner launched (HBO) Max, Paramount created Paramount+, and so on. Each company wants to keep its own movies and shows exclusive to its own platform. It's what convinces people to subscribe. The downside is that movies disappear from other services once the licensing rights move back to their original owners. And it's not just movies and TV. Sports have become just as fragmented. The Champions League is on one platform, Formula 1 on another, the NBA somewhere else. And even if you're lucky enough to subscribe to the right service, the movie you want might be on Hulu in the US, Disney+ in Germany or simply unavailable where you live.

As a student I don't have unlimited money. Three streaming subscriptions at around €8 or €10 each might not sound like much, but they add up quickly. If you want 4k or need more than one device, that's another extra charge. 

Subscription growth eventually slowed down, so streaming companies started looking for new ways to increase revenue. That's when ad-supported plans became the norm. The cheaper tiers include ads, while the ad-free versions keep getting more expensive. So you start with the ad-supported plan, eventually get tired of the worse experience, and the premium plan is the only option left.

And sports subscriptions are even worse. If you want to follow everything related to your favorite sport, spending €50 a month isn't unusual. Add regular price increases on top of that, and suddenly streaming isn't the affordable alternative it used to be.

Let's say you've accepted that this is just how streaming works now. Now you have to figure out where your movie actually is. Every app has its own interface, its own search function, and its own way of organizing content. Personally, I've never liked how clunky TV apps often feel. I'm looking at you, Prime (mostly the old Prime).

Eventually you find your movie. After it's over, another interesting recommendation pops up, so you save it to your watchlist. Except every platform has its own watchlist. Sure, every company wants you to stay in its own ecosystem. I get it. But before you know it, you've got movies saved across six different apps. Video quality isn't consistent either. One service includes 4K, while another only offers HDR or Full HD unless you pay more. And sometimes it's almost ridiculous: you watch the first movie on Disney+, then have to switch to another service just to watch the sequel.

There are more movies available than ever before, yet it's somehow harder to find one to watch. At least for me, it's not unusual to spend twenty minutes scrolling through different apps just to watch a ninety-minute movie. Sometimes, after all that searching, I don't even feel like watching anything anymore. And the recommendation algorithms. I mean, they've suggested some genuinely great movies to me so I don't want to pretend that they're completely useless. But a lot of the time they keep pushing the same popular titles.

Ironically, streaming is starting to look a lot like TV again. Ads are back unless you pay for the expensive tier. FAST channels with fixed schedules are becoming more common, because they're cheap to operate and generate advertising revenue 24/7. Bundles like Disney+, Hulu and ESPN+ feel a lot like cable TV all over again. Exclusive licensing means certain shows only exist on one platform. And live events, like boxing on Netflix or exclusive sports broadcasts elsewhere, are bringing appointment viewing back.

But it has consequences. Some people end up turning back to piracy. It's not just because they don't want to pay. A lot of them are already paying for several streaming services. The bigger issue is the convenience. Spotify showed that if legal streaming is more convenient than piracy, most people are happy to pay. Illegal platforms usually put everything into one interface. There's no jumping between apps, no wondering who owns the rights this month and regional restrictions often disappear. Of course there's a reason it's called piracy, but you get me. For every piracy site that is taken down, two more will pop up.

There are probably a few ways to improve things. Cross-platform search should be standard by now. A shared watchlist across services would help. You can track movies with Letterboxd or one of the other apps, but then you're relying on yet another app to organize your entertainment. Bundled subscriptions where you choose three services for a discounted price could also make things simpler. Those are just a few ideas, but they're probably easier said than done.

Streaming is still great, don't get me wrong. The flexibility is unmatched, and the picture quality has never been better. The problem isn't with streaming itself. It's just that the ecosystem became too fragmented. Streaming solved TV's biggest problems. But over time, it slowly recreated most of them. And that's why I'll probably keep asking myself the same question for a while longer: Wait… which streaming service was that on again?

sentences.zh: 一项服务包括 4K，而另一项服务仅提供 HDR 或全高清，除非您支付更多费用。

sentences.en: And sometimes it's almost ridiculous: you watch the first movie on Disney+, then have to switch to another service just to watch the sequel.
sentences.zh: 有时这几乎是荒谬的：你在 Disney+ 上观看第一部电影，然后必须切换到另一个服务才能观看续集。

blocks.html_en: There are more movies available than ever before, yet it's somehow harder to find one to watch. At least for me, it's not unusual to spend twenty minutes scrolling through different apps just to watch a ninety-minute movie. Sometimes, after all that searching, I don't even feel like watching anything anymore. And the recommendation algorithms. I mean, they've suggested some genuinely great movies to me so I don't want to pretend that they're completely useless. But a lot of the time they keep pushing the same popular titles.

sentences.en: There are more movies available than ever before, yet it's somehow harder to find one to watch.
sentences.zh: 可供观看的电影比以往任何时候都多，但找到一部可看的电影却变得更加困难。

sentences.en: At least for me, it's not unusual to spend twenty minutes scrolling through different apps just to watch a ninety-minute movie.
sentences.zh: 至少对我来说，花二十分钟浏览不同的应用程序只是为了看一部九十分钟的电影并不罕见。

sentences.en: Sometimes, after all that searching, I don't even feel like watching anything anymore.
sentences.zh: 有时，经过所有的搜索，我什至不想再看任何东西了。

sentences.en: And the recommendation algorithms.
sentences.zh: 以及推荐算法。

sentences.en: I mean, they've suggested some genuinely great movies to me so I don't want to pretend that they're completely useless.
sentences.zh: 我的意思是，他们向我推荐了一些真正很棒的电影，所以我不想假装它们完全没用。

sentences.en: But a lot of the time they keep pushing the same popular titles.
sentences.zh: 但很多时候他们不断推出同样的热门游戏。

blocks.html_en: Ironically, streaming is starting to look a lot like TV again. Ads are back unless you pay for the expensive tier. FAST channels with fixed schedules are becoming more common, because they're cheap to operate and generate advertising revenue 24/7. Bundles like Disney+, Hulu and ESPN+ feel a lot like cable TV all over again. Exclusive licensing means certain shows only exist on one platform. And live events, like boxing on Netflix or exclusive sports broadcasts elsewhere, are bringing appointment viewing back.

sentences.en: Ironically, streaming is starting to look a lot like TV again.
sentences.zh: 讽刺的是，流媒体又开始变得很像电视了。

sentences.en: Ads are back unless you pay for the expensive tier.
sentences.zh: 除非您支付昂贵的费用，否则广告会回来。

sentences.en: FAST channels with fixed schedules are becoming more common, because they're cheap to operate and generate advertising revenue 24/7.
sentences.zh: 具有固定时间表的 FAST 频道变得越来越普遍，因为它们的运营成本低廉，并且可以 24/7 产生广告收入。

sentences.en: Bundles like Disney+, Hulu and ESPN+ feel a lot like cable TV all over again.
sentences.zh: 像 Disney+、Hulu 和 ESPN+ 这样的捆绑包感觉又很像有线电视了。

sentences.en: Exclusive licensing means certain shows only exist on one platform.
sentences.zh: 独家授权意味着某些节目仅存在于一个平台上。

sentences.en: And live events, like boxing on Netflix or exclusive sports broadcasts elsewhere, are bringing appointment viewing back.
sentences.zh: Netflix 上的拳击比赛或其他地方的独家体育赛事直播等现场活动正在恢复预约观看。

blocks.html_en: But it has consequences. Some people end up turning back to piracy. It's not just because they don't want to pay. A lot of them are already paying for several streaming services. The bigger issue is the convenience. Spotify showed that if legal streaming is more convenient than piracy, most people are happy to pay. Illegal platforms usually put everything into one interface. There's no jumping between apps, no wondering who owns the rights this month and regional restrictions often disappear. Of course there's a reason it's called piracy, but you get me. For every piracy site that is taken down, two more will pop up.

sentences.en: But it has consequences.
sentences.zh: 但这是有后果的。

sentences.en: Some people end up turning back to piracy.
sentences.zh: 有些人最终又回归盗版。

sentences.en: It's not just because they don't want to pay.
sentences.zh: 这不仅仅是因为他们不想付钱。

sentences.en: A lot of them are already paying for several streaming services.
sentences.zh: 他们中的许多人已经在为多种流媒体服务付费。

sentences.en: The bigger issue is the convenience.
sentences.zh: 更大的问题是便利性。

sentences.en: Spotify showed that if legal streaming is more convenient than piracy, most people are happy to pay.
sentences.zh: Spotify 表明，如果合法流媒体比盗版更方便，那么大多数人都乐意付费。

sentences.en: Illegal platforms usually put everything into one interface.
sentences.zh: 非法平台通常将所有内容都放在一个界面中。

sentences.en: There's no jumping between apps, no wondering who owns the rights this month and regional restrictions often disappear.
sentences.zh: 应用程序之间没有跳跃，也不用想知道本月谁拥有权利，而且区域限制通常会消失。

sentences.en: Of course there's a reason it's called piracy, but you get me.
sentences.zh: 当然，这被称为盗版是有原因的，但你明白我的意思。

sentences.en: For every piracy site that is taken down, two more will pop up.
sentences.zh: 每关闭一个盗版网站，就会另外出现两个盗版网站。

blocks.html_en: There are probably a few ways to improve things. Cross-platform search should be standard by now. A shared watchlist across services would help. You can track movies with Letterboxd or one of the other apps, but then you're relying on yet another app to organize your entertainment. Bundled subscriptions where you choose three services for a discounted price could also make things simpler. Those are just a few ideas, but they're probably easier said than done.

sentences.en: There are probably a few ways to improve things.
sentences.zh: 可能有几种方法可以改进。

sentences.en: Cross-platform search should be standard by now.
sentences.zh: 跨平台搜索现在应该是标准的。

sentences.en: A shared watchlist across services would help.
sentences.zh: 跨服务的共享监视列表会有所帮助。

sentences.en: You can track movies with Letterboxd or one of the other apps, but then you're relying on yet another app to organize your entertainment.
sentences.zh: 您可以使用 Letterboxd 或其他应用程序之一跟踪电影，但随后您将依赖另一个应用程序来组织您的娱乐。

sentences.en: Bundled subscriptions where you choose three services for a discounted price could also make things simpler.
sentences.zh: 捆绑订阅（您可以以折扣价选择三项服务）也可以让事情变得更简单。

sentences.en: Those are just a few ideas, but they're probably easier said than done.
sentences.zh: 这些只是一些想法，但说起来容易做起来难。

blocks.html_en: Streaming is still great, don't get me wrong. The flexibility is unmatched, and the picture quality has never been better. The problem isn't with streaming itself. It's just that the ecosystem became too fragmented. Streaming solved TV's biggest problems. But over time, it slowly recreated most of them. And that's why I'll probably keep asking myself the same question for a while longer: Wait… which streaming service was that on again?

sentences.en: Streaming is still great, don't get me wrong.
sentences.zh: 流媒体仍然很棒，请不要误会我的意思。

sentences.en: The flexibility is unmatched, and the picture quality has never been better.
sentences.zh: 灵活性无与伦比，图像质量也前所未有。

sentences.en: The problem isn't with streaming itself.
sentences.zh: 问题不在于流媒体本身。

sentences.en: It's just that the ecosystem became too fragmented.
sentences.zh: 只是生态系统变得过于分散。

sentences.en: Streaming solved TV's biggest problems.
sentences.zh: 流媒体解决了电视的最大问题。

sentences.en: But over time, it slowly recreated most of them.
sentences.zh: 但随着时间的推移，它慢慢地重建了其中的大多数。

sentences.en: And that's why I'll probably keep asking myself the same question for a while longer: Wait…
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么我可能会在一段时间内继续问自己同样的问题：等等……

sentences.en: which streaming service was that on again?
sentences.zh: 又是哪个流媒体服务？

content_html_zh:
我是一个超级电影迷。 我什至称自己为电影迷。 我最喜欢的电影是那些让我事后思考的电影。 我事先不想考虑的是我可以在哪里真正观看它们。 如今，电影遍布数百种不同的流媒体服务。 我仍然不明白为什么没有更好的内置解决方案。 或者也许我只是不想接受这是唯一的选择。 不管怎样，我想弄清楚我们是如何来到这里的，所以我做了一些研究。

流媒体最初的承诺很简单。 只要您需要，一切都可以使用。 你可以在晚上坐下来狂看一整季，而不必等待下一集一周。

出色地... 每周发布似乎正在卷土重来。 我忘记了不止一个节目，只是因为我不得不等待一周才能看到下一集。 但总的来说，流媒体仍然改变了我们观看电影和电视的方式。

我仍然记得录制电视节目，否则你会错过它们，或者等到晚上 8:15。 （黄金时段）因为那是本周的大电影开始的时间。 今天，您只需躺在床上，打开应用程序，然后观看您想看的任何内容。 广播时间表不再重要。

另一个巨大的改进是没有广告。 电视中最烦人的事情之一是每十五分钟就有一次广告插播。 早期的流媒体没有这个。 您支付了 Netflix 订阅费用，仅此而已。

它通常也比有线电视或付费电视便宜，尽管老实说我不确定今天是否仍然如此。 也许最大的优势是一个流媒体服务就足够了。 人们想看的大多数电影和节目都在同一个地方，因此您不必考虑谁拥有版权。

然后一切开始支离破碎。 随着时间的推移，感觉每个主要工作室都推出了自己的服务。 随后，Netflix 证明了流媒体订阅的利润是多么可观。 电影公司意识到他们可以保留内容、推出自己的平台并自行收取订阅费，而不是将电影授权给 Netflix。 从商业角度来看，这是完全有道理的。 从观众的角度来看，这是混乱的开始。 迪士尼制作了Disney+，华纳推出了(HBO) Max，派拉蒙制作了Paramount+，等等。 每家公司都希望将自己的电影和节目保留在自己的平台上。 这就是说服人们订阅的原因。 缺点是，一旦许可权移回原始所有者，电影就会从其他服务中消失。 这不仅仅是电影和电视。 体育运动也变得支离破碎。 冠军联赛在一个平台上，一级方程式赛车在另一个平台上，NBA 在其他地方。 即使您足够幸运，订阅了正确的服务，您想要的电影也可能在美国的 Hulu 上、德国的 Disney+ 上，或者在您居住的地方根本无法观看。

作为一名学生，我没有无限的钱。 三个流媒体订阅的价格约为 8 或 10 欧元，听起来可能不多，但加起来很快。 如果您想要 4k 或需要多个设备，则需要额外付费。

订阅增长最终放缓，因此流媒体公司开始寻找新的方法来增加收入。 从那时起，广告支持的计划就成为常态。 更便宜的版本包含广告，而无广告版本则越来越贵。 因此，您从广告支持的计划开始，最终厌倦了更糟糕的体验，而高级计划是唯一的选择。

体育订阅的情况更糟。 如果您想关注与您喜爱的运动相关的所有内容，每月花费 50 欧元并不罕见。 除此之外，加上定期的价格上涨，突然之间，流媒体不再像以前那样是一种负担得起的选择。

假设您已经接受这就是流媒体现在的工作方式。 现在你必须弄清楚你的电影实际上在哪里。 每个应用程序都有自己的界面、自己的搜索功能以及自己的内容组织方式。 就我个人而言，我从来不喜欢电视应用程序的笨拙感觉。 我正在看着你，Prime（主要是老Prime）。

最终你找到了你的电影。 结束后，会弹出另一个有趣的推荐，因此您将其保存到您的观看列表中。 但每个平台都有自己的监视列表。 当然，每家公司都希望你留在自己的生态系统中。 我得到它。 但在您意识到之前，您已经在六个不同的应用程序中保存了电影。 视频质量也不稳定。 一项服务包括 4K，而另一项服务仅提供 HDR 或全高清，除非您支付更多费用。 有时这几乎是荒谬的：你在 Disney+ 上观看第一部电影，然后必须切换到另一个服务才能观看续集。

可供观看的电影比以往任何时候都多，但找到一部可看的电影却变得更加困难。 至少对我来说，花二十分钟浏览不同的应用程序只是为了看一部九十分钟的电影并不罕见。 有时，经过所有的搜索，我什至不想再看任何东西了。 以及推荐算法。 我的意思是，他们向我推荐了一些真正很棒的电影，所以我不想假装它们完全没用。 但很多时候他们不断推出同样的热门游戏。

讽刺的是，流媒体又开始变得很像电视了。 除非您支付昂贵的费用，否则广告会回来。 具有固定时间表的 FAST 频道变得越来越普遍，因为它们的运营成本低廉，并且可以 24/7 产生广告收入。 像 Disney+、Hulu 和 ESPN+ 这样的捆绑包感觉又很像有线电视了。 独家授权意味着某些节目仅存在于一个平台上。 Netflix 上的拳击比赛或其他地方的独家体育赛事直播等现场活动正在恢复预约观看。

但这是有后果的。 有些人最终又回归盗版。 这不仅仅是因为他们不想付钱。 他们中的许多人已经在为多种流媒体服务付费。 更大的问题是便利性。 Spotify 表明，如果合法流媒体比盗版更方便，那么大多数人都乐意付费。 非法平台通常将所有内容都放在一个界面中。 应用程序之间没有跳跃，也不用想知道本月谁拥有权利，而且区域限制通常会消失。 当然，这被称为盗版是有原因的，但你明白我的意思。 每关闭一个盗版网站，就会另外出现两个盗版网站。

可能有几种方法可以改进。 跨平台搜索现在应该是标准的。 跨服务的共享监视列表会有所帮助。 您可以使用 Letterboxd 或其他应用程序之一跟踪电影，但随后您将依赖另一个应用程序来组织您的娱乐。 捆绑订阅（您可以以折扣价选择三项服务）也可以让事情变得更简单。 这些只是一些想法，但说起来容易做起来难。

流媒体仍然很棒，请不要误会我的意思。 灵活性无与伦比，图像质量也前所未有。 问题不在于流媒体本身。 只是生态系统变得过于分散。 流媒体解决了电视的最大问题。 但随着时间的推移，它慢慢地重建了其中的大多数。 这就是为什么我可能会在一段时间内继续问自己同样的问题：等等…… 又是哪个流媒体服务？
